mission.title: Install and Configure Capsule on Kubernetes
mission.description: Capsule is a multi-tenancy and policy-based framework for Kubernetes that simplifies the management of multiple namespaces within a single cluster. By using Capsule, you can efficiently group namespaces into tenants and enforce policies, making it easier to manage resources and security across your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Capsule Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Capsule Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration. This allows you to install the Capsule Operator using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add capsule https://ghcr.io/projectcapsule/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Capsule Operator
steps.description:
Next, install the Capsule Operator in a dedicated namespace. This command will create the namespace if it doesn't already exist.
```bash
helm install capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --create-namespace --version 0.12.4
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Capsule Operator pod is running successfully. This command will list all pods in the capsule-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace capsule-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for the Capsule Operator
steps.description:
To ensure that the Capsule Operator has appropriate resource limits, you can edit the values.yaml file or pass resource limits directly during installation. Here’s an example of how to set resource limits:
```bash
helm upgrade capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --set global.jobs.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.jobs.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Capsule
steps.description:
Ensure that the necessary Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions are in place for the Capsule Operator. You can apply the following RBAC configuration:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: capsule-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ['']
  resources: ['pods', 'pods/log']
  verbs: ['get', 'list', 'watch']
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Capsule will result in the Capsule Operator running in the 'capsule-system' namespace, with its pods in a 'Running' state. You should also have resource limits configured and RBAC permissions set up for the operator.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add capsule https://ghcr.io/projectcapsule/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --set global.jobs.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set global.jobs.resources.limits.memory=512Mi

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: capsule-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ['']
  resources: ['pods', 'pods/log']
  verbs: ['get', 'list', 'watch']
EOF

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Capsule Operator Helm release from the cluster. This will remove the deployment and associated resources created by the Helm chart.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with Capsule to ensure a clean uninstallation.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the namespace used for Capsule and verify that all resources have been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Capsule installation to prevent data loss.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Capsule Operator to the latest version. Replace '0.12.5' with the desired version if needed.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Capsule Operator pod is running the new version successfully. This command will list all pods in the capsule-system namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description: If the Capsule Operator pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors. This may indicate a misconfiguration or resource issue.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description: If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that the version specified is compatible with the existing installation. You can check the current version with:

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description: If you encounter RBAC permission errors, verify that the necessary roles and bindings are correctly configured. Check the current RBAC settings with:

troubleshooting.title: Namespace not found
troubleshooting.description: If you receive an error indicating that the namespace 'capsule-system' does not exist, ensure that the namespace was created successfully during installation. You can check existing namespaces with:

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that Helm and kubectl are installed on your local machine.
